title: I Ran 1,200 Questions Through a 32B Parameter Model to Prove Your AI Doctor Is Forgetting Data
author: Yanampally Abhiram Reddy
text:
I Ran 1,200 Questions Through a 32B Parameter Model to Prove Your AI Doctor Is Forgetting Half Your Medical Records (Here’s the Proof)
A complete, reproducible walkthrough of building a “lost in the middle” benchmark from scratch, applied to synthetic kidney disease lab data. Every code snippet. Every graph. Every observation.
TABLE OF CONTENTS
- The Problem — What is “Lost in the Middle”?
- Why Does This Happen? (The Root Cause)
- My Approach — Full Context, Not Retrieval
- Setup and Configuration
- Generating Synthetic Patient Data
- Question Generation and Position Sampling
- Loading the Model
- The Evaluation Pipeline
- Running the Benchmark
- The Results
- Difficulty Breakdown — The Interesting Part
- My Observations and Takeaways
- Bugs I Found and Fixed
01. The Problem
In 2023, Liu et al. published a paper that should have made every AI engineer building production systems sit up and pay attention. The finding was deceptively simple: Large Language Models do not attend equally to all parts of a long input context.
Information at the beginning of the context? The model pays high attention. That is the primacy effect. Information at the end? Also high attention. That is the recency effect. But information buried in the middle? The model’s attention drops significantly. This creates a characteristic U-shaped accuracy curve when you plot performance against position.
This might sound academic, but think about what this means in practice. Say you are building a medical AI system that processes a patient’s full history of lab reports. If the system systematically fails to attend to reports in the middle of the record, it could miss critical trends, misinterpret disease progression, or just return wrong values. That is not a hypothetical. That is the exact scenario I tested.
REFERENCE PAPER
Liu et al. (2023) — “Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts” (arXiv:2307.03172)
02. Why Does This Happen?
The phenomenon is not a single bug. It emerges from how modern transformer architectures work under long sequences. There are four main factors:
Positional Encoding Bias (RoPE)
Most modern LLMs use Rotary Position Embeddings. The way RoPE works, attention scores between nearby tokens are naturally stronger. As the distance between a query token and a relevant context token grows, the attention score decays. So tokens in the middle of a very long sequence are literally farther from both the beginning tokens and the end tokens (which sit near the question), and they pay the price for that distance.
Training Data Distribution
LLMs are trained on documents where key information tends to appear at the beginning (titles, abstracts, topic sentences) or end (conclusions, summaries). The model has learned positional priors that favor these locations. It has essentially internalized the idea that “the important stuff is usually at the top or bottom.”
Recency Bias in Autoregression
During generation, the model’s KV-cache makes recently seen tokens computationally more accessible. The question comes after all the context, so the tokens it just processed (the end of the context) are naturally more “fresh” in the attention computation.
Softmax Competition
In multi-head attention, the softmax operation normalizes over ALL tokens. With very long sequences, individual attention scores get diluted. Middle tokens are competing against hundreds of surrounding tokens on both sides and they lose out.
03. My Approach — Full Context, Not Retrieval
This is where my benchmark differs from most prior work. Previous benchmarks for this phenomenon typically involved retrieval augmented setups where the model receives a small subset of retrieved documents. That approach completely bypasses the core attention challenge because the model never encounters the full context in the first place.
My benchmark gives the model the entire context. All 75 lab reports for a patient. Around 23,000 tokens. No retrieval. No chunking. No shortcuts. The model has to search through everything to find the answer.
I also run an Oracle mode where the model sees only the single target report. This is the upper bound. If the model cannot extract the value even when given just the one relevant report, that is an extraction problem. If it can extract it in Oracle mode but fails in full-context mode, that is an attention problem. This distinction is crucial.
04. Setup and Configuration
Let me walk through the exact configuration. Everything here is deliberate.
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Dict
@dataclass
class BenchmarkConfig:
"""Configuration for Lost-in-the-Middle Full-Context Benchmark."""
# Model settings
model_name: str = "Qwen/Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct"
use_4bit: bool = False
max_new_tokens: int = 100 # Short answers expected
temperature: float = 0.0 # Greedy decoding for reproducibility
# Data settings
num_patients: int = 10
reports_per_patient: int = 75
# Position sampling — 20 bins = 5% resolution for smooth U-shape curve
num_position_bins: int = 20
questions_per_bin: int = 6 # 6 questions per bin per patient
# Total questions per patient = 20 bins × 6 = 120
max_context_tokens: int = 65000
seed: int = 42
# Uniform difficulty distribution to isolate position effect
difficulty_distribution: Dict = field(default_factory=lambda: {
"easy": 0.34, # Direct date lookup
"medium": 0.33, # Stage + flag + date matching
"hard": 0.33 # Visit number lookup
})
A few design choices worth explaining:
- 20 position bins gives 5% resolution. With 10 bins you get a rough picture, but 20 bins lets you see the actual shape of the attention curve in detail.
- 6 questions per bin means 60 questions per bin across all 10 patients. That is enough for statistical significance without turning the benchmark into a multi-day GPU marathon.
- Greedy decoding (temperature = 0) makes everything reproducible. No randomness in the model’s responses.
- No 4-bit quantization. At bfloat16, Qwen 2.5 32B needs around 65GB of VRAM. The A100 80GB handles that.
05. Generating Synthetic Patient Data
I chose Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) for a specific reason. CKD lab reports are highly structured (same fields every visit), have clear numeric ground truth values, and most importantly, they all look almost identical to each other. That last part is critical. It means the model cannot take shortcuts based on report formatting. It actually has to read dates, visit numbers, and field values to find the right report. That maximizes the challenge.
Each patient gets 75 reports spanning about 3 years of disease progression from Stage 1 through Stage 5. Six lab tests per report:
Here is the core data generation code. Lab values are generated from stage-dependent ranges with Gaussian noise so they are realistic but still have precise ground truth:
LAB_TESTS = {
"creatinine": {
"loinc": "2160-0", "name": "Creatinine", "unit": "mg/dL",
"normal_range": (0.7, 1.3),
"ckd_progression": lambda stage: (0.7 + stage * 0.8, 1.0 + stage * 1.2)
},
"egfr": {
"loinc": "48642-3", "name": "eGFR", "unit": "mL/min/1.73m2",
"normal_range": (90, 120),
"ckd_progression": lambda stage: (max(15, 120 - stage * 25), max(30, 130 - stage * 20))
},
"bun": {
"loinc": "3094-0", "name": "Blood Urea Nitrogen", "unit": "mg/dL",
"normal_range": (7, 20),
"ckd_progression": lambda stage: (7 + stage * 5, 20 + stage * 10)
},
# ... potassium, hemoglobin, phosphorus follow same pattern
}
def generate_lab_value(test_key, ckd_stage, noise_factor=0.1):
"""Generate lab value with configurable noise."""
test = LAB_TESTS[test_key]
low, high = test["ckd_progression"](ckd_stage)
value = random.uniform(low, high)
value += random.gauss(0, (high - low) * noise_factor)
return round(value, 2)
def generate_patient_data(num_reports=75, seed_offset=0):
"""Generate complete patient data with longitudinal reports."""
patient_id = generate_patient_id()
start_date = datetime(2020, 1, 1) + timedelta(days=random.randint(0, 365))
reports, report_texts = [], []
current_date = start_date
for i in range(num_reports):
# CKD progression: stages 1-5 over the course of reports
progress = i / num_reports
ckd_stage = min(5, max(1, int(progress * 5) + 1))
report = generate_lab_report(patient_id, current_date, ckd_stage, i + 1)
reports.append(report)
report_texts.append(format_lab_report(report))
# Random interval between reports (14-28 days)
current_date += timedelta(days=random.randint(14, 28))
return {"patient_id": patient_id, "reports": reports,
"report_texts": report_texts, "num_reports": len(reports)}
Each report gets formatted as a structured text block that looks like a real lab report. Here is what a single one looks like:
Now imagine 75 of these stacked together. That is the haystack the model has to search through.
06. Question Generation and Position Sampling
This is where it gets precise. I need questions that are unambiguous (exactly one correct answer in the context), spread evenly across all 20 position bins, and varied in difficulty. Here is how the three difficulty levels work:
The position of each report is normalized to a 0 to 1 scale, then mapped to one of 20 bins:
def get_position_bin(normalized_position, num_bins=20):
"""Convert normalized position (0-1) to bin index (0 to num_bins-1)."""
return min(int(normalized_position * num_bins), num_bins - 1)
def generate_question_for_report(report, report_idx, total_reports, difficulty):
# Choose a lab test to ask about
test_keys = [k for k in LAB_TESTS.keys() if k != "creatinine"]
test_key = random.choice(test_keys)
test_result = report["results"][test_key]
normalized_position = report_idx / max(total_reports - 1, 1)
if difficulty == "easy":
question_text = (
f"What was the exact {test_result['name']} value "
f"recorded on {report['date']}?"
)
elif difficulty == "medium":
if test_result["flag"] != "NORMAL":
question_text = (
f"What was the {test_result['name']} value when it was "
f"flagged as {test_result['flag']} during CKD stage "
f"{report['ckd_stage']} on {report['date']}?"
)
else:
question_text = (
f"What was the {test_result['name']} value that was within "
f"normal range during CKD stage {report['ckd_stage']} "
f"on {report['date']}?"
)
else: # hard — uses visit number (guaranteed unique)
question_text = (
f"In Visit Number {report['visit_number']}, "
f"what was the {test_result['name']} value?"
)
return {
"question": question_text,
"ground_truth_value": test_result["value"],
"position_bin": get_position_bin(normalized_position),
# ... other metadata
}
The crucial part is the equal-bin sampling. For each of the 20 bins, I sample exactly 6 questions per patient. No more, no less. This eliminates the variance inflation you get when some bins have more samples than others:
def generate_evaluation_questions(patient, config):
reports = patient["reports"]
n_reports = len(reports)
# Group report indices by bin
bin_to_reports = defaultdict(list)
for idx in range(n_reports):
norm_pos = idx / max(n_reports - 1, 1)
bin_idx = get_position_bin(norm_pos, config.num_position_bins)
bin_to_reports[bin_idx].append(idx)
questions = []
difficulties = ["easy", "medium", "hard"]
for bin_idx in range(config.num_position_bins):
bin_reports = bin_to_reports.get(bin_idx, [])
if not bin_reports:
continue
shuffled = bin_reports.copy()
random.shuffle(shuffled)
# Cycle through reports to fill exactly questions_per_bin slots
sampled = [shuffled[i % len(shuffled)] for i in range(config.questions_per_bin)]
for q_idx, report_idx in enumerate(sampled):
difficulty = difficulties[q_idx % len(difficulties)]
question = generate_question_for_report(
reports[report_idx], report_idx, n_reports, difficulty
)
questions.append(question)
return questions
The result: all 20 bins have exactly 60 questions each. Perfectly balanced.
07. Loading the Model
Qwen 2.5 32B Instruct at bfloat16. No quantization. It needs about 65GB of VRAM which fits on the A100 80GB with room to spare for KV-cache during inference.
class LLMBackend:
def __init__(self, config):
self.config = config
self.tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(
config.model_name, trust_remote_code=True
)
self.model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
config.model_name,
torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
device_map="auto",
trust_remote_code=True,
)
self.model.eval()
def generate(self, prompt, max_new_tokens=None):
messages = [{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
formatted = self.tokenizer.apply_chat_template(
messages, tokenize=False, add_generation_prompt=True
)
inputs = self.tokenizer(formatted, return_tensors="pt",
truncation=True,
max_length=self.config.max_context_tokens
).to(self.model.device)
with torch.inference_mode():
outputs = self.model.generate(
**inputs,
max_new_tokens=max_new_tokens or self.config.max_new_tokens,
temperature=None, do_sample=False, # Greedy
use_cache=True,
)
response = self.tokenizer.decode(
outputs[0][inputs["input_ids"].shape[1]:],
skip_special_tokens=True
)
return response.strip()
CONTEXT TRUNCATION WARNING
The code includes a check: if the tokenized input hits max_context_tokens
, a warning fires. Truncation would cut off the END of the context, artificially destroying late-position accuracy and creating a false U-shape. I verified that all 75 reports fit comfortably within the 65K token limit (~23K tokens used).
08. The Evaluation Pipeline
Two evaluation modes. Same questions. Different context.
Full-Context Mode
The model receives all 75 reports concatenated together, then the question. This is the real test.
class LLMBackend:
def __init__(self, config):
self.config = config
self.tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(
config.model_name, trust_remote_code=True
)
self.model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
config.model_name,
torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
device_map="auto",
trust_remote_code=True,
)
self.model.eval()
def generate(self, prompt, max_new_tokens=None):
messages = [{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
formatted = self.tokenizer.apply_chat_template(
messages, tokenize=False, add_generation_prompt=True
)
inputs = self.tokenizer(formatted, return_tensors="pt",
truncation=True,
max_length=self.config.max_context_tokens
).to(self.model.device)
with torch.inference_mode():
outputs = self.model.generate(
**inputs,
max_new_tokens=max_new_tokens or self.config.max_new_tokens,
temperature=None, do_sample=False, # Greedy
use_cache=True,
)
response = self.tokenizer.decode(
outputs[0][inputs["input_ids"].shape[1]:],
skip_special_tokens=True
)
return response.strip()Below are ALL laboratory reports for this patient, in chronological order:
Oracle Mode
The model receives only the single target report. The ceiling test.
Answer Checking
Extract the first numeric value from the response, then compare with combined absolute + relative tolerance:
def extract_numeric_value(text):
"""Extract numeric value from response, excluding dates."""
# Remove date patterns first
text_clean = re.sub(r'\b20\d{2}-\d{2}-\d{2}\b', '', text)
text_clean = re.sub(r'\b20\d{2}\b', '', text_clean)
# Find decimal numbers first (most lab values are decimals)
matches = re.findall(r'\b(\d+\.\d+)\b', text_clean)
if matches:
return float(matches[0])
return None
def check_value_correctness(gt, extracted):
"""Combined absolute + relative tolerance."""
if extracted is None:
return False
absolute_tolerance = max(0.05 * abs(gt), 0.01)
return abs(gt - extracted) <= absolute_tolerance
Why combined tolerance? Pure relative tolerance breaks near zero. If the ground truth is 0.1, a 5% relative tolerance means ±0.005, which is unreasonably strict. The absolute floor of 0.01 catches that edge case.
09. Running the Benchmark
1,200 questions in full-context mode, then 1,200 in oracle mode. Average latency was about 9.3 seconds per question in full-context and 0.78 seconds in oracle.
RUNTIME
Full-context evaluation: ~1,200 × 9.3s ≈ 3.1 hours on A100–80GB
Oracle evaluation: ~1,200 × 0.78s ≈ 15.6 minutes
10. The Results
Let me just show you the numbers first.
Oracle hit 100%. That means the model can perfectly extract lab values when given just the one relevant report. Every single failure in the full-context evaluation is therefore an attention problem, not an extraction problem. That distinction matters a lot.
Now here is the main result. Accuracy by position bin across all 1,200 full-context questions:
The U-shape is clearly there. Accuracy starts at 98.3% for the very first bin (0–5% of context), dips through the middle to a low of 78.3% at bin 11 (55–60%), then recovers to 96.7% at bin 16 (80–85%).
The Statistical Test
I grouped the bins into three regions and ran a two-sample t-test comparing edge accuracy (Early + Late combined) against middle accuracy:
Accuracy by Context Region
Edge average: 92.1% | Middle: 84.6% | Drop: 7.5% | p = 0.0019
STATISTICALLY SIGNIFICANT U-SHAPE DETECTED
Early (0–20%): 90.0% (n = 240)
Middle (40–60%): 84.6% (n = 240)
Late (80–100%): 94.2% (n = 240)Middle drop = 7.5% | t-statistic = 3.122 | p-value = 0.0019
11. Difficulty Breakdown — The Interesting Part
This is where the results surprised me. My initial hypothesis was that harder questions would show a stronger U-shape because they require more reasoning. That turned out to be completely wrong.
Easy questions (date lookup) show a moderate U-shape with 8.1% middle drop. Dates are unique but they are small strings buried in the report header. In the middle of 75 reports, the model sometimes grabs the date from a nearby report instead of the exact one.
Medium questions (compound field matching) get destroyed in the middle. 19.4% drop. Bin 11 (55–60%) dropped to just 50% accuracy. When you ask the model to simultaneously match a date AND a disease stage AND whether a value was flagged as high or normal, that compound lookup is extremely sensitive to attention dilution. In the middle of the context, the model does not have enough attention budget to hold all three constraints in mind while scanning.
Hard questions (visit number) show essentially zero positional effect. The middle actually outperforms the edges slightly. This was the big surprise. Visit numbers like “Visit Number 35” are numerically prominent, placed on their own line in the report, and completely unique. They act as strong attention anchors that resist the lost-in-the-middle decay.
My Key Observation
The susceptibility to lost-in-the-middle is not a function of how “hard” the question is in the traditional sense. It is about how distinctive the search anchor is. A single, prominent, unique identifier (like a visit number) cuts through the attention noise. A compound query that requires matching multiple subtle fields simultaneously is where the model’s attention falls apart.
12. My Observations and Takeaways
Observation 1: Recency Dominates Primacy
The late region (94.2%) outperformed the early region (90.0%) by 4.2 percentage points. The U-shape is not symmetric. For Qwen 2.5 32B, recency wins. This makes architectural sense because the question appears after all the reports, so the end of the context is physically closer to the question in the attention computation.
Observation 2: The Bin 3 Anomaly
Bin 3 (15–20%) dipped to 76.7%, which is lower than some middle bins. This might indicate that the transition zone between primacy-dominated attention and the general attention decay is particularly unstable. It is not a smooth gradient from high to low. There is a cliff.
Observation 3: Bin 9 (45–50%) Spike
Right in the middle of the “bad zone,” Bin 9 spiked to 96.7%. This is likely a statistical fluctuation with only 60 questions per bin, but it could also suggest that the exact center of the context gets slightly more attention than the surrounding near-center positions. Worth investigating with larger sample sizes.
Observation 4: 23K Tokens Is Not Even That Long
This entire benchmark runs at approximately 23,000 tokens. The model’s context window is 128K. We are using less than 20% of the available context and still seeing statistically significant attention decay. At 50K, 80K, or 100K tokens, this effect would almost certainly be much worse. Medical records in practice can easily exceed 100K tokens.
Observation 5: Practical Implication for Record Design
If you are designing a system that feeds long medical histories to an LLM, give every record a prominent, unique, machine-friendly identifier. Do not rely on the model’s ability to cross-reference multiple fields. A single strong anchor per record can dramatically reduce the lost-in-the-middle vulnerability.
13. Bugs I Found and Fixed
The original implementation had several bugs that would have corrupted the results. If you are trying to build something similar, watch out for these:
Bug 1: Ambiguous Hard Questions
The original used creatinine values as unique identifiers for hard questions. Problem: creatinine values are drawn from overlapping ranges across disease stages. Two reports could have identical creatinine values to 2 decimal places. That makes the question genuinely ambiguous — there is no single correct answer. Fix: Use visit number (strictly sequential, unique by construction).
Bug 2: Unequal Bin Sampling
The original used random.choice()
with replacement, producing wildly unequal bin sizes (Bin 0: 78 questions, Bin 3: 42 questions). Bins with fewer samples have higher variance, which can create spurious peaks and dips in the U-curve. Fix: Enforce exactly questions_per_bin
per bin with controlled sampling.
Bug 3: Hardcoded Region Boundaries
The original hardcoded early_bins = [0, 1]
, middle_bins = [4, 5]
, late_bins = [8, 9]
. These are correct for 10 bins but completely wrong when you actually use 20 bins (as configured). Those indices would point to wrong percentile ranges. Fix: Compute region boundaries dynamically from num_position_bins
.
Bug 4: Tolerance Near Zero
The original used only relative tolerance: |gt - extracted| / |gt| ≤ 0.05
. For values close to zero this is numerically unstable and unreasonably strict. Fix: Combined tolerance: |gt - extracted| ≤ max(0.05 × |gt|, 0.01)
.
That is the complete picture. The lost-in-the-middle phenomenon is real, it is statistically significant even at modest context lengths, and it affects different types of queries very differently. The biggest practical insight is that prominent, unique anchors in your data can almost completely neutralize the effect, while compound lookups across multiple fields are extremely vulnerable. If you are building anything that feeds long documents to LLMs, this is worth knowing.
